preFix.title: Wooden boards for bookbinding
preFix.subtitle: Selecting, cutting, and preparing timber boards for long-life and decorative bindings
preFix.excerpt: Wooden boards predate binders board by centuries and remain the strongest, most stable option for large-format or archival bindings. The preparation involves selecting straight-grained timber, cutting with the grain, and bevelling the edges before attachment.
preFix.sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on wooden boards. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. Cockerell distinguishes oak, beech, and lime as suitable timber species and specifies grain direction and bevelling.

content.text: The codex form was bound in wooden boards for its first thousand years. Oak, beech, lime, and elm all appear in surviving early bindings. The reasons are practical: timber is stable, holds lacing channels, takes tooled decoration, and resists the stress of a heavy text block over centuries. Binders board replaced wood in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries because it is lighter, cheaper, and easier to cut. But for large-format books, bindings intended to last generations, or decorative work where the board texture contributes to the aesthetic, timber boards remain the better material.

attrs.body: When cutting timber boards with a craft knife, use a steel rule and apply multiple light scoring passes rather than one heavy cut. The blade skids under heavy pressure on dense wood. Change the blade more frequently than you would for paper work.
attrs.heading: Safety: cutting tools

content.text: What you need
Selecting the timber
Straight-grained, knot-free timber is essential. Knots create hard spots that split under the stress of lacing channels and cause the board to warp over time. The
board grain
must run parallel to the spine, exactly as paper grain does in binders board. A board cut across the grain will cup or warp within a year of closing.

Lime (Tilia) is the most workable of the traditional binding timbers: close-grained, stable, and easy to carve. Oak is stronger but harder to cut and bevel by hand. Beech is mid-range. For home binding, a piece of straight-grained lime or poplar 4 to 6 mm thick works well for A5 and A4 books. Avoid pine and softwoods: the grain is too uneven and the wood compresses differently across the grain versus along it.
Cutting the boards

Mark the board dimensions with a steel rule and pencil. The board should be 3 mm taller than the text block (1.5 mm overhang at head and tail) and 3 mm wider (3 mm overhang at the foredge, no overhang at the spine edge).

Score along the marked line with a craft knife and steel rule, six or more times, increasing pressure with each pass. The wood fibres need to be cut through progressively. A single heavy cut splits the grain rather than cutting it.
Snap the board along the scored line with both hands, supporting the length you are keeping. The scored line creates a clean break.
Bevelling the edges
The
bevel

is cut along the inside edges of the board: the spine edge and the head, tail, and foredge edges. The bevel tapers the board thickness from full depth at the outer face to roughly half depth at the inner edge. Sand the bevel with 120-grit sandpaper wrapped around a flat block, angling the block at 45 degrees, then refine with 240-grit. The bevel stops the leather covering from showing a sharp ridge at the board edge.
Cutting the lacing channels
Wooden boards attach to the text block by
lacing channels

. Mark two channels per board, each 15 mm from the top and bottom sewing cords respectively. The channel runs from the spine edge of the board inward for 25 mm, then angles slightly to exit on the inner face. Score the channel groove with the bookbinding awl, working along the inner face. The cord end passes through the spine-edge hole, travels along the channel, and emerges at the inner-face exit where it is hammered flat.
Final preparation

Sand both faces of the board with 240-grit paper, working along the grain. The face that will show under the covering needs only light work; the inner face needs smooth flatness so the pastedown adheres evenly. Wipe away all dust with a slightly damp cloth and let dry before covering.

items.fix: Store the warped board under heavy weights between damp blotting paper for 48 hours, then re-dry under light weights. For future boards, always verify grain direction before cutting.
items.cause: Grain running across rather than along the spine, or the board was not fully dry before covering.
items.symptom: Board warps after the book is finished

items.fix: Reinforce the split with a drop of PVA, clamp for an hour, then re-score the channel at a shallower angle.
items.cause: The awl was pushed at too steep an angle or the wood has a hidden run of short grain.
items.symptom: The lacing channel splits open along the wood grain
